LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE DEC-28-2023 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT TOYOTA MOTOR SALES, U.S.A., INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL BINDING ARBITRATION, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANTS TOYOTA MOTOR SALES, U.S.A., INC. ("TMS") AND JEH ENTERPRISES, INC. DBA SAN FRANCISCO TOYOTA'S ("DEALER") MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS GRANTED IN PART. THE COURT COMPELS ARBITRATION OF SEMONIAN'S CLAIMS AGAINST DEALER. THE COURT STAYS THIS PROCEEDING, INCLUDING SEMONIAN'S CLAIMS AGAINST TMS, PENDING THE ARBITRATION. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS.) COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH COMPLIES WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: JOSEPH M. QUINN; CLERK: S. KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of John W. Myers Iv
Off Calendar Dec-28-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT TOYOTA MOTOR SALES, U.S.A., INC.'s Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc. ("TMS") and JEH Enterprises, Inc. dba San Francisco Toyota's ("Dealer") motion to compel arbitration is granted in part.

Plaintiff Paul Semonian purchased a vehicle from Dealer in 2022, executing a contract which included an agreement to arbitrate governed by the Federal Arbitration Act ("FAA"). Semonian filed a complaint in 2023 alleging violations of the Consumer Legal Remedies Act and Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act, and conversion.

To order arbitration under the FAA, the court must find (1) an agreement to arbitrate exists, (2) the dispute falls within the scope of that agreement, and (3) the agreement is enforceable. (Chiron Corp. v. Ortho Diagnostic Sys., Inc. (9th Cir. 2000) 207 F.3d 1126, 1130.) First, an arbitration agreement exists here between signatories Semonian and Dealer only. (Mot. Exh. A.) The court rejects nonsignatory TMS's argument that it is covered by the agreement under the doctrine of equitable estoppel. (Mot. 6:10-12; Ochoa v. Ford Motor Company (2023) 89 Cal.App.5th 1324, 1333 [manufacturer's claims were not "intimately founded in and intertwined" with the underlying contract obligations].) Second, as to scope, the agreement covers all claims arising from the purchase of the vehicle. Because this dispute concerns misrepresentations at the time of purchase regarding the vehicle's fuel tank range, this dispute falls within the scope of the agreement. The claims regarding nonsignatory TMS, however, do not fall within the scope of this agreement because these claims are not intertwined with the sales contract. (Ochoa, supra, 89 Cal.App.5th at 1333; Kielar v. Superior Court (2023) 94 Cal.App.5th 614, 620; Montemayor v. Ford Motor Co. (2023) 92 Cal.App.5th 958, 971 (all disagreeing with Felisilda v. FCA US LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 486).) Third, the agreement is enforceable as to the signatory parties, and Semonian does not argue otherwise. In sum, only Semonian and Dealer are compelled to arbitration.

The court compels arbitration of Semonian's claims against Dealer. The court stays this proceeding, including Semonian's claims against TMS, pending the arbitration. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 1281.2; see also Code Civ. Proc., 1281.4; 9 U.S.C. Sec. 3.) To the extent the court has discretion to manage the proceeding differently, the court exercises its discretion in favor of a stay pending arbitration of the arbitral claims. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 1281.2.) The court rejects Plaintiff's request that the arbitration be stayed pending the outcome of the court action.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
